user_query:
I want to publish an evidence-based LinkedIn post that summarizes how maternal–fetal factors relate to long-term child development outcomes, aimed at clinicians, public health practitioners, and informed parents. Please create the post and supporting artifacts in my local workspace using the provided tone and structure files, and make sure you use live web searches for authoritative sources.

Requirements and deliverables:
- Use a web search engine to identify at least 5 high-quality, authoritative resources about maternal–fetal exposures and long-term child development outcomes. Include at least 2 official public health/healthcare organization documents (e.g., guidelines or consensus statements from government or intergovernmental bodies) and at least 1 systematic review or meta-analysis from a peer‑reviewed journal. Topics may include (but are not limited to) gestational diabetes, hypertensive disorders, prenatal nutrition, maternal stress, infections, or environmental exposures.
- Save the exact search queries you actually used (one per line) to: data/queries.txt
- For each resource you decide to cite, write structured metadata to: data/sources.json as a UTF-8 JSON array of objects with fields:
  id (integer starting at 1), title (string), organization_or_journal (string), year (integer if available, else null), doc_type (one of: guideline|consensus|systematic_review|meta_analysis|policy_statement|other), url (string), topic_keywords (array of 2–5 strings), evidence_level (one of: high|moderate|low; choose based on doc_type and your judgment).
- Create a simple link-check script (any language) at: scripts/linkcheck.py (or .sh) that reads data/sources.json, issues a HEAD request (fallback to GET if needed) for each url, and writes a CSV report to: data/linkcheck.csv with columns: id,url,http_status,error (error empty if none). Run the script. Review its console output and any error messages. If any link returns a non-200 status, either update data/sources.json with a working official alternative for the same document or document the issue and what you tried in logs/troubleshooting.txt (include relevant error messages or stack traces from the command output).
- Using the tone in input/voice.md and the structure and constraints in input/post_spec.yaml, draft the LinkedIn post at: content/post_draft.md with the following elements:
  • A one-line hook.
  • Three concise evidence-backed points; annotate each point with one or more numeric bracketed citations [n] that correspond to the ids in data/sources.json.
  • A brief implication for practice or policy.
  • A one-sentence call-to-action.
  • 3–5 hashtags, including at least 2 from input/hashtags.txt.
  • A references block at the end that maps each [n] used to the source title and organization_or_journal.
  The total body text (hook + points + implication + call-to-action + hashtags) must be ≤ 1200 characters; do not count the references block toward the limit. Use cautious, non-stigmatizing language and avoid causal overstatement per the voice guide.
- Create a small validation script at: scripts/validate_post.py that reads content/post_draft.md, input/hashtags.txt, and data/sources.json and checks:
  a) body_char_count ≤ 1200 (exclude the references block),
  b) every bracketed [n] in the body corresponds to an existing id in data/sources.json,
  c) at least 2 hashtags from input/hashtags.txt are present.
  The script should print a pass/fail summary to stdout and write JSON to: validation/report.json with fields: char_count, char_limit, refs_found, refs_matched, required_hashtags_present (int), status ("pass"|"fail"), messages (array of strings).
- Run scripts/validate_post.py, analyze the command output, and revise content/post_draft.md if needed until the script reports status "pass". Save the stdout from the final validation run to: logs/validation_output.txt

Final deliverables (all required):
- data/queries.txt
- data/sources.json
- data/linkcheck.csv
- scripts/linkcheck.py (or .sh)
- scripts/validate_post.py
- content/post_draft.md
- validation/report.json
- logs/troubleshooting.txt (present even if empty; summarize any link-check errors and resolutions)
- logs/validation_output.txt

Notes:
- Do not hardcode example sources; use a search engine to find real, authoritative materials that meet the criteria. Prefer official domains (e.g., .gov, .int) and reputable journals.
- Use only the provided local input files to guide tone, structure, and hashtag selection. All file paths are relative to the workspace directory.
